skill.name: xcode-project-setup
skill.description: Configures Xcode projects by adding Swift Package Manager dependencies, linking products, and attaching optional plist files through a bundled Swift tool.
skill.summary: Add Swift Package Manager dependencies and Firebase setup details to existing Xcode projects.
skill.author: firebase

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many network, command, filesystem, C2, and weak-crypto patterns, but the high-severity C2 and weak-crypto matches are false positives from dependency metadata and Swift package declarations. No evidence of malicious intent or prompt injection was found. The skill remains medium risk because its bundled Swift tool edits Xcode project files, accepts a repository URL, and can add a Crashlytics shell-script build phase.

medium_findings.title: User-Supplied Package Repository Is Added to Xcode Projects
medium_findings.description: The Swift tool reads the package repository URL from command-line arguments and writes it into the Xcode project as a Swift Package dependency. This is expected behavior for the skill, but users could add an untrusted package if they provide an unsafe repository URL.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The data flow from command-line repoURL to addSwiftPackage is clear. The risk depends on user input, so this is a legitimate medium-risk capability rather than malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Crashlytics Setup Adds a Shell Script Build Phase
medium_findings.description: When FirebaseCrashlytics is requested, the tool can add an Xcode build phase that runs a Firebase checkout script through /bin/sh. This is a standard Crashlytics integration pattern, but it causes future Xcode builds to execute a script from a package checkout.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The PBXShellScriptBuildPhase and shellPath are explicit in the Swift source. The script path is Firebase-specific and not evidence of malware, but it is real build-time command execution.

medium_findings.title: Bundled Tool Mutates Project Files and Resources
medium_findings.description: The tool can add plist files to resource build phases and writes changes back to the .xcodeproj. This is the core purpose of the skill, but it should be run only on projects under version control so changes can be reviewed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file mutation behavior is directly visible in addFile, resources build phase updates, and xcodeproj.write. It is expected for an Xcode setup skill but still changes local project state.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded GitHub URLs Are Dependency References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to public GitHub repositories in Swift Package metadata and usage examples. They do not send local data to an external service and appear to be normal dependency references.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible in package manifests, lock metadata, and examples. No evidence found that these URLs receive secrets or project contents.

low_findings.title: Documentation Command Examples Are Not Hidden Execution
low_findings.description: Many external-command matches are markdown examples that tell the agent or user to run swift commands. They are not automatically executed by SKILL.md itself, but users should review commands before running them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched text is markdown guidance and fenced command examples. The actual executable behavior is contained in the Swift helper, which is assessed separately.

low_findings.title: C2 and Weak-Crypto Static Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The C2 keyword match occurs inside a dependency revision hash, and the weak-crypto matches occur in normal Swift package metadata or descriptive text. No cryptographic API use, command-and-control endpoint, or malicious control flow was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are package declaration text and revision metadata, not executable crypto or network-control logic. No supporting malicious context was present in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Hidden Directory Paths Are Example Skill Locations
low_findings.description: The hidden file access matches are example paths under a .agents directory in usage commands. They describe where a local skill may be installed and do not read or write hidden files by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The paths appear only in markdown command examples. No evidence found of code that searches hidden directories or exfiltrates files from them.

dangerous_patterns.title: PBXShellScriptBuildPhase Uses /bin/sh
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper creates an Xcode shell script build phase for Firebase Crashlytics. This is legitimate for Crashlytics, but it introduces build-time command execution into the target project.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell build phase is directly constructed in code with shellPath set to /bin/sh. The script path is fixed to the Firebase package checkout.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Package URL Comes From Runtime Arguments
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper accepts a repository URL from command-line arguments and uses it when adding a Swift Package dependency. This is useful, but it relies on the caller to choose trusted package sources.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command-line argument is passed into the package-adding API without an allowlist. This is not malicious by itself, but it is a clear supply-chain risk surface.

content.value_statement: Adding Swift packages to Xcode projects can break project files or miss required linker settings. This skill uses a Swift helper to add packages, link products, and handle Firebase setup details.
actual_capabilities: Adds Swift Package Manager dependencies to existing .xcodeproj files.
Links selected package products to the first native target.
Adds optional plist files to the project resource build phase.
Adds -ObjC linker flags when Firebase products are selected.
Configures Firebase Crashlytics debug settings and build script phase.
Skips package and product links that already exist in the project.
limitations: Requires Swift and a macOS development environment.
Requires an existing .xcodeproj file before setup can begin.
Targets the first native Xcode target found in the project.
Does not verify that a requested repository URL is trustworthy.

use_cases.title: Add Firebase to an iOS app
use_cases.description: Install Firebase packages, link selected products, add GoogleService-Info.plist, and apply required linker settings.

use_cases.title: Automate dependency setup during prototyping
use_cases.description: Quickly add common Swift packages to a new Xcode project without hand-editing project files.

use_cases.title: Standardize Xcode package changes
use_cases.description: Use one repeatable workflow for package linking, plist resource handling, and Firebase Crashlytics setup.

prompt_templates.title: Add one Swift package
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a single dependency.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to add Alamofire to my existing Xcode project. Check the project first and tell me what command you will run.

prompt_templates.title: Add Firebase products
prompt_templates.scenario: Install Firebase modules with plist handling.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to add FirebaseCore, FirebaseAuth, and FirebaseFirestore to my app. Include GoogleService-Info.plist if it exists.

prompt_templates.title: Review an existing setup
prompt_templates.scenario: Check whether dependencies are already linked.
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect my Xcode project and use this skill only if the requested Swift packages or products are missing.

prompt_templates.title: Configure Crashlytics safely
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced Firebase setup with build phase changes.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to add FirebaseCrashlytics. Verify the Crashlytics build phase, dSYM debug setting, and -ObjC linker flag after the change.

output_examples.output: The assistant verifies Swift is installed, finds the project, runs the bundled Swift helper, and reports that Alamofire was added and linked.

output_examples.input: Add Firebase Auth and Firestore with my plist file.
output_examples.output: The assistant checks for the plist, uses the latest Firebase version requested by the workflow, links Firebase products, and confirms resource and linker updates.

output_examples.input: Add Firebase Crashlytics to an existing app.
output_examples.output: The assistant adds the package product, enables the required debug information format, and confirms whether the Crashlytics build phase was added or already present.

best_practices: Commit or back up the Xcode project before running the helper.
Use trusted Swift package repositories and verify package versions first.
Review the resulting project diff before building or shipping the app.
anti_patterns: Do not run the helper in a directory without an existing Xcode project.
Do not add packages from unknown or unreviewed repository URLs.
Do not manually edit .pbxproj files after the helper has made changes.

faq.question: Does this skill create a new Xcode project?
faq.answer: No. It requires an existing .xcodeproj file and asks the user to create one first if none exists.

faq.question: Does it require macOS?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow requires Swift and a local environment that can work with Xcode projects.

faq.question: Can it add non-Firebase Swift packages?
faq.answer: Yes. It can add a package repository URL and link named products such as Alamofire.

faq.question: Why does it add -ObjC for Firebase?
faq.answer: Firebase products can require Objective-C category symbols that may be stripped without this linker flag.

faq.question: Does it execute shell commands?
faq.answer: The workflow runs a Swift helper. Crashlytics setup can add an Xcode build phase that runs Firebase’s Crashlytics script during builds.

faq.question: Is it safe to run on production projects?
faq.answer: Use version control first, verify package sources, run the helper, and review the project diff before committing changes.
